name: GOG
gradeReason: DRM-free installers you can back up and run forever, with no client required.
whatBuyMeans: You download an installer that works without GOG's permission, servers, or client — forever. This is the closest digital gets to actually owning a game.
canRevoke: Games do get delisted from sale (licensing), but installers you already own keep working and remain re-downloadable. Even if GOG died tomorrow, your backed-up installers survive it.
refunds: 30 days, even after downloading and playing — the most generous refund window in the industry.
offlineStory: Everything works offline by definition. GOG Galaxy is optional.

name: Steam
gradeReason: Reliable and consumer-friendly in practice, but you own nothing on paper, and everything hangs on one account.
whatBuyMeans: The Subscriber Agreement is explicit: you buy a license ('subscription'), not a product. California law now forces the checkout page to admit it.
canRevoke: An account ban — or a payment dispute gone wrong — can cut you off from your entire library at once. Valve rarely does it arbitrarily, but 'rarely' is a policy, not a right. Your library also legally dies with you: it cannot be inherited or transferred.
refunds: Under 2 hours played and within 14 days, largely automatic. Better than consoles, worse than GOG.
offlineStory: Offline mode works for most games but needs periodic re-authentication, and DRM-heavy titles ignore it. Valve keeps most delisted games playable — its long-term record here is genuinely good.

incidents.text: Replaced CS:GO — a game 25 million people played — in place with Counter-Strike 2.
Started showing 'you are buying a license, not a game' at checkout after California's AB 2426.

name: Epic Games Store
gradeReason: Standard license terms, always-online tendencies, and a young store with a thin track record — but a decent record when it kills its own games.
whatBuyMeans: A revocable license tied to your Epic account, per the standard EULA.
canRevoke: Account termination removes library access. Free-game hoarding doesn't protect you: no account, no games.
refunds: 14 days / under 2 hours, similar to Steam. Self-refund flow exists.
offlineStory: Many titles work offline, but the launcher's offline mode is notoriously flaky, and some giveaways carry third-party DRM anyway.

incidents.text: When Epic killed Paragon it refunded every purchase — and did the same for Rumbleverse in 2023.

name: PlayStation Store
gradeReason: License-only ownership plus an actual track record of removing purchased content and strict refund rules.
whatBuyMeans: A license Sony can modify or revoke. Sony's ToS is explicit that content can become unavailable.
canRevoke: It's not theoretical: Sony announced removal of purchased Discovery TV seasons in 2023 (reversed only after outcry), and account bans sever access to every digital purchase on the account.
refunds: 14 days — but only if you haven't downloaded or streamed the content. Downloading it, which the console often does automatically, voids the refund. Among the most hostile refund policies in gaming.
offlineStory: Digital games need periodic license validation; one console can be 'primary' for offline play. Disc games mostly work offline — when the disc actually contains the game.

incidents.text: Announced deletion of purchased Discovery content from user libraries; reversed after public outcry.
Tried to close the PS3/Vita stores, which would have stranded thousands of digital-only games; partially reversed.
Concord: bought copies became unplayable 14 days after launch (fully refunded).

name: Xbox Store
gradeReason: Same license-only model as PlayStation, softened by better refunds, good backward compatibility — and hard evidence of what store closures look like (Xbox 360, 2024).
whatBuyMeans: A revocable license under the Microsoft Services Agreement, spanning console and PC.
canRevoke: Account enforcement can cut off digital libraries. The Xbox 360 Store's closure in July 2024 shows the lifecycle: your purchases stay re-downloadable 'for now', new purchases are gone forever.
refunds: 14-day self-service refunds that are generally more forgiving than Sony's; still discretionary.
offlineStory: One console can be your 'home' Xbox for offline play of digital titles. Game Pass titles are rentals by design — a fifth of your library can leave every month.

incidents.text: Closed the Xbox 360 Store, ending new purchases for a whole generation's catalog.
Forza Horizon 4 delisted with all DLC — the fourth Horizon in a row to vanish from sale.

name: Nintendo eShop
gradeReason: The industry's most restrictive digital policies, a fresh history of closing whole stores, and physical releases that are increasingly empty boxes.
whatBuyMeans: A license bound to your Nintendo Account and console. Nintendo's terms allow it to make content unavailable; its 2025 EULA update even claims the right to brick devices over some violations.
canRevoke: The Wii U/3DS eShops closed in 2023 — over a thousand digital-only games became unpurchasable overnight, with re-downloads promised only 'for the foreseeable future'. Wii Shop purchases are already gone for good.
refunds: Effectively none. All sales final except where local law forces otherwise. Pre-orders can't be cancelled in the final 7 days.
offlineStory: Cartridge games with the full game on-card work forever — the gold standard. But 'game-key cards' on Switch 2 are download codes in a box, and digital titles need periodic online checks if you use multiple consoles.

incidents.text: Closed the Wii U and 3DS eShops, stranding 1,000+ digital-only titles.
Terminated online services for every Wii U and 3DS game at once.
Launched Switch 2 with 'game-key cards' — physical boxes containing no game.

name: EA App
gradeReason: A mandatory client from the publisher with gaming's most crowded graveyard, wrapped around licenses it can end at will.
whatBuyMeans: A revocable license under EA's User Agreement, which EA can terminate for a broad range of conduct — taking your whole EA library with it.
canRevoke: Account termination is total: every EA game, gone. EA also retired Origin outright in 2025, forcibly migrating users and dropping 32-bit systems.
refunds: 24 hours after first launch, 14 days from purchase — one of the tighter windows, self-service where available.
offlineStory: An offline mode exists but many titles phone home anyway, and EA's own service-updates page is a running list of games it has switched off.

incidents.text: Anthem switched off — one of ten EA games shut down in 2026 alone.

name: Physical discs & cartridges
gradeReason: The last form of game ownership that doesn't need anyone's permission — where it still actually contains the game.
whatBuyMeans: You own the object, and no server outage or account ban can take a complete-on-disc game from you. First-sale rights mean you can sell, lend, or bequeath it.
canRevoke: Nobody can revoke a cartridge. But a disc that's a download stub, or a game with always-online DRM (Anthem discs are landfill now), dies with its servers anyway.
refunds: Standard retail return policies — and a resale market digital stores will never allow.
offlineStory: Check before you buy: 'game-key card' or 'download required' on the box means the physical copy preserves nothing. Complete-on-media releases (most Nintendo carts, some PS5 discs, boutique labels like Limited Run) are the real thing.

incidents.text: Anthem discs became unplayable — physical media is only as durable as the servers behind it.
